summary: Martin argues that programming language evolution has been driven primarily by Moore's Law - the exponential growth in computing power that allowed us to move from assembly to interpreted languages. With clock speeds plateauing around 2010 and core density growth slowing, he suggests we may have entered a stable era where language proliferation will slow. The anticipated functional programming revolution, driven by fears of many-core processors, may never fully materialize since we're still waiting for 8+ core laptops. Martin welcomes this stability as a transition from the 'adolescence' of software development to an era of professional craftsmanship with a settled complement of languages and tools.
key_points: Moore's Law drove language evolution from assembly to interpreted languages by making computational resources effectively infinite
Clock speeds plateaued at ~2.8GHz over a decade ago, and core density growth has slowed to incremental rather than exponential
The anticipated many-core processor revolution that would have demanded functional programming hasn't materialized
The 'crisis' driving functional language adoption may not occur, meaning Java/C#, Ruby/Python, and Go may remain sufficient
The industry may be entering a stable 'adult' period with fewer new languages and more focus on craftsmanship
money_quotes: We, who used to worry about microseconds, now cavalierly push systems to production written in languages that execute in textual interpreters!
Most of us work in an environment of virtual infinities. There may be some constraints out there somewhere, but most of us know we will never bump into them.
That's the way it is with limits – often you don't see them coming until you hit them.
If Moore's law was the driver of our language evolution, what will drive it now?
I, for one, look forward to the end of the barnstorming era and the onset of the era of professional, and ethical craftsmanship.
An era where we discard the detritus of the excesses of our youth, and settle upon a small complement of languages, platforms, and frameworks.
key_insight: The end of Moore's Law may herald a welcome stabilization in software development, shifting focus from chasing new languages to mastering professional craftsmanship with proven tools.
